root: ^Mount Niphates rises from the eastern edge of Eden like a broken tooth against the sky. Its peak is barren — stripped clean by wind and time — and from its summit the whole of Paradise lies spread below like a map drawn by God's own hand.

^He has left the garden below. He has left Adam and Eve sleeping in their bower. He has climbed through the night, driven upward by something he cannot name — a pressure in his chest, a burning behind his eyes that is not fire but something worse.

^The sun is rising. The first light catches the peaks of the eastern mountains, painting them gold and rose. Below, Eden stirs — birds beginning their dawn chorus, rivers catching the light, flowers opening to greet the morning.
^It is, Satan thinks, the most beautiful thing he has ever seen.

the_address: ^He turns to face the sun — that blazing eye of Heaven that sees all things, that lit his way when he was still Lucifer, the Morning Star.
^O thou that with surpassing glory crowned, look'st from thy sole dominion like the god of this new world — at whose sight all the stars hide their diminished heads —
^His voice cracks on the words. He steadies himself against the rock.
^To thee I call, but with no friendly voice, and add thy name, O Sun, to tell thee how I hate thy beams, that bring to my remembrance from what state I fell, how glorious once above thy sphere...
^The confession pours out of him like blood from a wound.
^(Let the grief consume him. He mourns what he was.)
^(Let him rage against God. The injustice still burns.)
^(Let him speak with cold clarity. He has moved beyond grief and rage.)

c-0: ^Till pride and worse ambition threw me down, warring in Heaven against Heaven's matchless King. Ah, wherefore? He deserved no such return from me — whom He created what I was in that bright eminence...
^Satan's knees buckle. He catches himself on the rock, hands scraping against stone. The archangel who led legions, who built Pandemonium, who crossed Chaos itself — brought to his knees by memory.

^He remembers the light. Not Hell's fire, not Eden's gentle sun, but the light of Heaven — that all-pervading radiance that was warmth and music and love all at once. He had bathed in it. He had been made of it.
^Lifted up so high, I 'sdained subjection, and thought one step higher would set me highest — and in a moment quit the debt immense of endless gratitude, so burdensome...
c-1: ^He created me what I was — and what was that? A servant. A singer of hymns. A bright and beautiful nothing, made to kneel and praise and never ask why.
^Satan's hands clench into fists. The old fire returns — the fire that lit the rebellion, that forged the weapons of war, that burned even in the Lake of Fire.
^Was it so great a crime to want more? To look upon the throne and think — I could sit there? To hear the praise of ten thousand angels and think — they could praise me?
^He paces the summit, his shadow stretching long in the early light. Below, a flock of birds rises from the garden in a spiral of wings.
^If He wanted obedience, He should not have made me capable of ambition. If He wanted worship, He should not have given me the wit to question it. The fault is in the making — not the made!
c-2: ^Satan's voice drops to something almost conversational. The grand rhetoric falls away. What remains is simple, terrible honesty.
^I knew what I was doing. I knew the cost. I weighed the throne of Heaven against the freedom of Hell, and I chose.
^He looks down at Eden — at the tiny figures of Adam and Eve just now stirring in their bower.
^And I would choose the same again. Not because I was right — perhaps I was wrong. Not because God is unjust — perhaps He is just. But because I am what I am, and what I am cannot kneel.
^He pauses, watching a eagle circle below him, riding the thermals that rise from the warm garden.
^That is my damnation. Not the fire. Not the darkness. The knowledge that I did this to myself, and that I would do it again, and again, and again, world without end.

the_question: ^The sun climbs higher. Its light is warm on Satan's face — a warmth he does not deserve and cannot enjoy. He shields his eyes and looks east, where the light is strongest.
^The question comes unbidden, rising from some depth he thought he had sealed shut.
^Is there no place left for repentance, none for pardon left?
^The words hang in the mountain air. For a moment, the wind stills. The birds fall silent. Even Eden seems to hold its breath.
^None left but by submission — and that word disdain forbids me, and dread of shame among the spirits beneath, whom I seduced with other promises and other vaunts than to submit...
^He laughs — a short, bitter sound that echoes off the rocks.
^(He almost repents. The door is still open, if only he could walk through it.)
^(He rejects repentance utterly. It was never truly an option.)
^(He sees repentance for what it is — another trap, another form of submission.)

c-0: ^For a moment — one crystalline, agonizing moment — Satan feels it. The possibility. The door that God, in His infinite mercy or infinite cruelty, has left ajar.

^He could kneel. Right here, on this mountaintop, with the sun on his face and Eden spread below. He could prostrate himself and cry out for forgiveness, and perhaps — perhaps — the sky would open, and the light would pour down, and he would be Lucifer again.

^But even as they do, he sees it — the future that repentance would bring. The return to Heaven. The resumed hymns. The sideways glances of angels who remember. The throne of God, still occupied, still demanding praise. And himself, kneeling, forever kneeling, wearing forgiveness like a chain.
^No. That was the last time. The door is closed now — not by God, but by me. And it will stay closed.
c-1: ^The laughter dies. Satan's face hardens into something ancient and immovable.
^Repentance? For what — for being what He made me? For seeing clearly when He demanded blindness? For wanting freedom when He offered only gilded chains?

^He steps to the very edge of the precipice. The wind catches his wings — those great, tattered, magnificent wings — and for a moment he looks like what he once was: the most beautiful thing in all creation.
^I will not repent. I will not kneel. I will not crawl back to that throne and beg for scraps of grace from the hand that cast me out. If that is pride, then pride is all I have, and I will keep it.
c-2: ^Satan considers the question as if it were a problem in mathematics. Repentance. Submission. Pardon. The words have a logical structure, and he follows it to its end.

^Say I could repent — say I kneel, and He forgives. What then? I return to Heaven. I resume my place. And how long before the same thoughts come again? How long before I look at the throne and feel the same hunger?
^He knows the answer. He has always known it.

^Repentance would not hold. Not because I am incapable of humility, but because the thing that drove me to rebellion is not a mood — it is my nature. God made me to desire greatness. He cannot unmake that without unmaking me. And if He unmakes me, what is there left to forgive?
^A faint smile crosses his face — the smile of a condemned man who has finally stopped hoping for a pardon.

the_declaration: ^The sun is fully risen now. Eden blazes below in all its impossible beauty — the rivers gleaming silver, the forests deep and green, the mountains crowned with light. It is a world made for joy, and Satan stands above it, locked out of joy forever.
^He speaks the words that will define him for all eternity.
^So farewell hope, and with hope farewell fear, farewell remorse! All good to me is lost.

^Each farewell is a door slamming shut. Hope — gone. Fear — gone. Remorse — gone. He is stripping himself of everything that might slow him down, everything that might make him hesitate when the moment comes.
^(This is Satan's tragedy — he knows exactly what he is losing.)
^(This is Satan's triumph — he refuses to be destroyed by what he cannot have.)

c-0: ^And he does know. That is the horror of it. Satan is not stupid. Satan is not mad. Satan sees Eden's beauty as clearly as God does. He understands love — he watched Adam and Eve and understood exactly what they have. He remembers Heaven — every note of every hymn, every ray of every dawn.
^He knows precisely what he is renouncing. And he renounces it anyway, because the alternative — submission, obedience, the slow suffocation of the self — is worse than any Hell.
^That is what makes Satan tragic. Not that he fell, but that he fell with his eyes open.
c-1: ^There is something magnificent in it. Something terrible and magnificent. The refusal to be broken. The insistence on remaining himself, even when himself is damned.

^God can take his light. God can take his home. God can cast him into fire and darkness and pain. But God cannot take his will. That is Satan's — the one thing that was never given and cannot be revoked.
^And as long as he has his will, he has not truly lost.

the_final_turn: ^Evil, be thou my good! By thee at least divided empire with Heaven's King I hold. By thee, and more than half perhaps will reign — as Man ere long, and this new world, shall know.

^The declaration echoes off the mountains. Below, Adam stirs in his sleep. Eve murmurs something and draws closer to him. They do not hear. They cannot imagine what crouches above them on the mountain, watching, planning, resolved.
^Satan descends from Mount Niphates. His step is steady now. His face is calm. The torment is not gone — it will never be gone — but it has been mastered, locked away behind a wall of iron will.
^He has a purpose now. He has a target. And he has stripped away every last scruple that might have stayed his hand.

^The descent takes him through groves of cedar and pine, past waterfalls that catch the morning light, through meadows where deer graze without fear. Eden opens before him like a book written in a language he can read but never speak.
^(He moves toward the forbidden tree, to study it, to understand God's one prohibition.)
^(He moves toward Eve, who has wandered from Adam to tend the morning flowers alone.)
^(He takes the form of a lesser creature — a toad — and crouches by Eve's ear as she sleeps.)

c-0: ^The Tree of Knowledge stands in a clearing at the garden's heart. It is not the tallest tree — that honour belongs to the Tree of Life — but it has a presence. Its bark is darker than the others. Its fruit hangs low, heavy, luminous in the morning light.
^Satan circles it slowly. He does not touch the fruit — not yet. He studies the tree as a siege engineer studies a fortress wall, looking for the weakness, the point of attack.
^One fruit. One act of disobedience. That is all it will take to unmake Paradise.
^Satan smiles. It is not a pleasant smile. But it is, for the first time in a long while, a genuine one.
c-1: ^Eve has risen early. She moves through the garden alone — Adam still sleeps in the bower — tending the roses that have grown wild overnight. She hums as she works, a melody that has no words because she has not yet learned that songs need them.

^Satan watches from the shadow of a fig tree. She is magnificent — not in the way of angels, not in the way of Heaven's terrible beauty, but in a softer, warmer way. She is alive in a manner that nothing in Heaven or Hell has ever been alive.

^And she is curious. He can see it in the way her eyes linger on the horizon, the way her fingers trace the petals of unfamiliar flowers, the way she glances — just once, just briefly — toward the centre of the garden where the forbidden tree stands.
^That glance is enough. Satan has found his way in.
c-2: ^Satan's form shimmers and contracts. The great wings fold in, the proud frame diminishes, the blazing eyes dim to something small and cold. Where an archangel stood, a toad now crouches — mottled, dark, unremarkable.
^He hops through the grass toward the bower where Eve lies sleeping. Adam breathes deeply beside her, one arm across her waist. Satan-as-toad crawls close to Eve's ear.

^And there, in the language of dreams, he begins to whisper. Images of the forbidden tree. The lustre of its fruit. The taste of knowledge. The promise of becoming more than what she is — not just a woman in a garden, but something closer to a god.
^Eve stirs. Her lips part. In her dream, she reaches for a fruit that glows like a star.
^Satan withdraws before dawn. The seed is planted. The rest will follow in time.

the_close: ^The morning wears on. Eden continues its eternal routine of beauty — the sun climbing, the flowers blooming, the rivers singing over their stones. Adam and Eve will go about their day as they always do, tending and naming and loving, unaware that anything has changed.
^But something has changed. The garden has an intruder now — a mind bent on its destruction, a will as hard as the stones of Hell, moving through Paradise with terrible purpose.
^Satan has made his choice. The soliloquy is over. What comes next is not words but action.
^And Eden's last peaceful morning draws toward its close.
